fact_candidates: Prepare a decision-grade research brief on grid-scale energy storage options for mid-sized cities for city planners evaluating a 2030 resilience roadmap.

Research mission: Identify which grid-scale energy storage technologies deserve deeper procurement analysis and which claims remain weakly evidenced. Treat the brief as a planning input for decisions over the next 18 months while considering infrastructure relevance through 2030.

Known context and limits: The city has rising summer peak demand, wildfire-related outage risk, limited downtown land, and political pressure to improve resilience without locking into immature technology. Missing location-specific inputs include load profiles, interconnection limits, land availability, tariff design, outage-duration targets, permitting constraints, critical loads, budget, ownership model, and political risk tolerance.
State live-search status, context status, verification status, and highest
impact missing inputs near the top.
Use a balanced source mix: public agencies, operators, deployments,
independent technical reviews, skeptical economic analysis, vendor claims
clearly labeled, recent news when recency matters, technical/legal/financial
If live search is unavailable, say so and produce a research plan plus a
provisional brief clearly marked as unverified.
Do not fabricate citations, URLs, dates, quotes, source names, source access,
Separate facts, reported claims, analysis, estimates, assumptions, and
Grade major claims by relevance, specificity, freshness, independence, and
End major findings with evidence grade, main gap, and
Compare alternatives using decision-relevant criteria rather than listing them
Start the comparison with leading option, runner-up, decisive criterion, and
Include confidence level, basis for confidence, strongest counterargument,
evidence that would change the conclusion, readiness category, assumptions,
limits, contradictions, open questions, next searches, and bottom line.
Produce these sections: research status; executive answer; source map;
comparative alternatives; main findings; contradictions and open questions;
implications for city planners and sequencing through 2030; next searches; and
